explanation: SET NOCOUNT OFF leaves row-count messages enabled for every statement, adding network chatter and confusing some clients.
SET XACT_ABORT OFF while the procedure uses explicit transactions allows run-time errors to leave a transaction open and partially applied.
SELECT * on Sales.Orders couples callers to all current and future columns, including wide nvarchar(MAX) comment columns.
SELECT TOP (10) from Sales.Orders without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary, nondeterministic set of rows.
DISTINCT on OrderID is redundant because OrderID is the primary key of Sales.Orders and already unique; it forces a needless sort/hash.
WITH (NOLOCK) on Sales.Orders can return uncommitted, missing, or duplicated rows due to dirty reads and allocation-order scans.
YEAR(o.OrderDate) in the predicate is non-sargable; use a date range (>= '20160101' AND < '20170101') to allow an index seek.
Comparing int column o.CustomerID with nvarchar variable @CustomerIdText forces an implicit conversion that can degrade the seek on the CustomerID index.
(@CustomerID IS NULL OR o.CustomerID = @CustomerID) defeats cardinality estimation and index seeks; use OPTION (RECOMPILE) or dynamic SQL for optional filters.
LIKE N'%urgent%' on o.Comments has a leading wildcard, preventing any index seek and forcing a scan of an nvarchar(MAX) column.
NOT IN against a subquery on Sales.Invoices.CustomerID returns no rows if the subquery ever yields NULL; prefer NOT EXISTS for safe semantics.
ISNULL(o.CustomerID, 0) = 1 wraps the indexed CustomerID column in a function, making the predicate non-sargable; CustomerID is NOT NULL so ISNULL is also redundant.
o.OrderID + 0 = @OrderID performs arithmetic on the primary key column, preventing a direct index seek; compare the column directly.
o.CustomerID <> 1 is an inequality predicate that typically scans large portions of Sales.Orders rather than seeking.
CROSS JOIN between Sales.Orders and Sales.Customers has no join predicate and multiplies the row counts of both tables.
SELECT TOP (1) over the Orders/Customers cross join has no ORDER BY, so the returned row is arbitrary.
Comma-separated FROM list of Sales.Orders and Sales.OrderLines with the join condition in WHERE; use explicit INNER JOIN ... ON syntax.
SELECT TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary matching Orders/OrderLines row.
The scalar COUNT(*) subquery against Sales.OrderLines executes per outer Orders row; rewrite as a GROUP BY join or OUTER APPLY.
Correlated EXISTS against Sales.OrderLines runs per outer row; it could be expressed as a semi-join/set join on OrderID with the Quantity filter.
The CASE WHEN o.Comments IS NULL expression is duplicated in the select list and ORDER BY; alias it once (e.g., via CROSS APPLY or ORDER BY the alias) to avoid divergence.
ORDER BY 2 refers to a select-list position and silently changes meaning if columns are reordered; order by o.OrderDate explicitly.
OFFSET/FETCH ordered only by o.OrderDate, which is not unique; add a tie-breaker such as o.OrderID to make paging stable.
UNION between branches filtered on OrderID = 1 and OrderID = 2 is disjoint by construction; UNION ALL avoids a needless duplicate-removal sort.
(SELECT COUNT(*) ...) > 0 counts all matching Sales.OrderLines rows when EXISTS would stop at the first match.
SELECT @OrderID = o.OrderID silently accepts multiple rows and keeps an arbitrary last value; use SET with a deterministic single-row query.
Table variable @OrderIds can receive fixed low cardinality estimates, producing poor plans if it holds non-trivial row counts; consider a temp table.
SELECT TOP (5) feeding @OrderIds has no ORDER BY, so which five OrderIDs are inserted is nondeterministic.
SELECT ... INTO #ImplicitOrders creates the temp table schema implicitly; declare the table explicitly for stable types and clarity.
SELECT TOP (5) into #ImplicitOrders has no ORDER BY, so the captured rows are arbitrary.
DECLARE/OPEN/FETCH cursor over Sales.Orders processes rows one at a time for work that can be expressed set-based.
The cursor's SELECT TOP (2) has no ORDER BY, so the cursor iterates arbitrary rows.
WHILE loop performs iterative row-by-row style processing instead of a single set-based operation.
Builds executable SQL by concatenating CONVERT(nvarchar(20), @CustomerID) into the string instead of binding a parameter.
EXEC (@Sql) executes concatenated SQL with no parameterization, preventing plan reuse and inviting injection patterns.
Concatenates @OrderID into the SQL text rather than passing it as an sp_executesql parameter.
sys.sp_executesql is called with only the SQL string; the OrderID value is embedded instead of bound via a parameter definition.
#Scratch is created explicitly but never dropped before the procedure ends, relying on implicit scope cleanup.
The CATCH block only SELECTs ERROR_MESSAGE(); it neither rethrows with THROW nor returns a failure status, so callers see success.
RAISERROR is used for a new error path; THROW provides correct severity/state semantics and preserves original error numbers.
PRINT output is buffered and unsuitable for structured diagnostics; use RAISERROR ... WITH NOWAIT or proper logging.
CustomerID = 1 embeds a business identifier directly in the query instead of accepting it as a parameter or lookup.
The unlabelled date constant '20160101' is embedded in query logic with no explanation of its business meaning.
CONVERT(date, '01/02/2016') without a style parameter is interpreted per session DATEFORMAT/language and can flip day and month.
COALESCE on both CustomerID columns in the join predicate prevents index seeks and normal join optimization between Orders and Invoices.
SELECT TOP (1) over the Orders/Invoices join has no ORDER BY, returning an arbitrary row.
Sales.Orders is self-joined on OrderID only to retrieve the same OrderID already available on the row.
SELECT TOP (1) on the self-join has no ORDER BY, so the returned row is arbitrary.
ORDER BY o.Comments sorts every order by an nvarchar(MAX) LOB column, causing an expensive, memory-heavy sort.
SUM(ol.Quantity) aggregates the entire Sales.OrderLines fact table with no restrictive filter, scanning every row.
HAVING ol.OrderID > 0 is a row-level predicate on the grouping column and should be in WHERE to filter before aggregation.
ORDER BY on a single-row aggregate (COUNT(*) without GROUP BY) is meaningless and adds a needless operator.
MERGE on Sales.Orders is used despite documented concurrency and correctness edge cases; use separate UPDATE/INSERT logic.
WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET target.Comments = target.Comments writes the current value back, incurring logging and locking with no change.
UPDATE ... SET Comments = Comments assigns the column its current value, generating log records and locks without changing data.
DELETE FROM Sales.OrderLines WHERE Quantity = -1 filters on a broad, non-key business value rather than a stable key, risking over-deletion.
WAITFOR DELAY '00:00:01' blocks a worker thread and holds session resources instead of using asynchronous scheduling.
BEGIN TRANSACTION is not wrapped in BEGIN TRY/BEGIN CATCH, so an error can leave the transaction open with locks held.
SET IDENTITY_INSERT Sales.Orders ON changes session identity state and creates fragile coupling to identity management.
EXEC sp_who calls a system routine without schema qualification; use sys.sp_who to avoid resolution overhead and ambiguity.
SET ROWCOUNT 1 is never reset to 0, silently limiting every subsequent SELECT/UPDATE/DELETE in the session to one row and causing incorrect results.
INSERT INTO #DemoCategories VALUES (...) omits an explicit column list, breaking silently if the table's column order or count changes.
The category identifier 99 is a hard-coded business value embedded in the INSERT rather than a parameter or lookup.
INSERT INTO #DemoCustomers relies on positional column mapping with no explicit column list, which is fragile to schema changes.
LIKE '%' + @CustomerName + '%' has a leading wildcard, preventing an index seek on the unique CustomerName index and forcing a scan.
SQL text is built by concatenating @CustomerName directly into the string instead of binding it as a parameter.
User-supplied @CustomerName is concatenated directly into the dynamic SQL string; a crafted value can escape the quotes and inject arbitrary SQL.
sp_executesql is invoked with only the concatenated string and no parameter definition, forfeiting parameterization and plan reuse.
sp_executesql is called without the sys. schema qualifier.
UPDATE #DemoCustomers SET CustomerCategoryID = 1 has no WHERE clause and overwrites every row in the table.
CustomerCategoryID = 1 embeds a business category identifier directly instead of using a parameter or lookup.
The WHERE predicate on o.OrderDate filters the outer-joined Sales.Orders table, silently converting the LEFT JOIN into an inner join and dropping customers without orders.
The unlabelled date constant '2016-01-01' is embedded in the filter with no indication of its business meaning.
DeliveryMethodID = NULL never evaluates to TRUE under ANSI NULL semantics; the query always returns zero rows. Use IS NULL.
Scalar UDF dbo.GetCustomerType(CustomerID) in the select list executes per row, serializing the plan and hiding costs.
The CATCH block handles an error raised inside an open transaction but never rolls back, potentially leaving the transaction open with locks held.
The CATCH block only selects ERROR_MESSAGE(); it neither rethrows with THROW nor returns a failure status, so the caller sees success.
WITH (INDEX = 1) hard-codes an index choice on Sales.Customers, breaking if indexes change and overriding the optimizer.
Three levels of nested derived tables (t1/t2/t3) obscure the query's intent; flatten or refactor to a CTE.
LIKE '%A%' on CustomerName has a leading wildcard, preventing an index seek on the unique CustomerName index.
SELECT * from dbo.GetCustomerOrdersMulti couples the caller to every column returned by the function.
dbo.GetCustomerOrdersMulti is a multi-statement TVF whose fixed cardinality estimate and lack of inlining hurt plan quality; prefer an inline TVF.
